past_medical_history: Type 2 diabetes mellitus, diagnosed 8 years ago, well-controlled on oral hypoglycemics
Hypertension, diagnosed 10 years ago, controlled on amlodipine
Remote history of mild traumatic brain injury from a motor vehicle accident 15 years ago, no loss of consciousness, no residual deficits at the time
Obstructive sleep apnea, diagnosed 4 years ago, uses CPAP nightly
family_history: Father died of myocardial infarction at age 70
Paternal uncle diagnosed with Parkinson's disease at age 75
No known family history of early-onset dementia or Alzheimer's disease

social_history.occupation: Retired civil engineer, stopped working 1 year ago due to difficulty with complex calculations
social_history.tobacco: Former smoker, 15 pack-years, quit 12 years ago
social_history.alcohol: Social drinker, 2-3 glasses of wine per week

social_history.driving: Still driving but wife reports he has gotten lost on familiar routes twice in the past 3 months

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person and place; oriented to month and year but not the exact date. Montreal Cognitive Assessment (MoCA) score 21/30: lost points on delayed recall (0/5), visuospatial/executive (cube copy, trail-making errors), and abstraction (1/2). Clock drawing mildly disorganized with incorrect hand placement. Serial 7s performed slowly with one error. Conversational speech fluent but with occasional word-finding pauses.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal, round, reactive to light. Visual fields full to confrontation bilaterally. Extraocular movements intact without nystagmus. Facial sensation and strength symmetric. Hearing grossly intact. Palate elevates symmetrically. Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius strength normal. Tongue midline without fasciculations.
neurological_exam.motor: Strength 5/5 in all extremities. No pronator drift. Muscle bulk and tone normal throughout.
neurological_exam.sensory: Intact to light touch, pinprick, vibration, and proprioception in all extremities. Cortical sensory function intact (graphesthesia, stereognosis).
neurological_exam.reflexes: 2+ and symmetric in biceps, triceps, brachioradialis, patellar, and Achilles tendons bilaterally. Plantar responses flexor bilaterally.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose and heel-to-shin testing intact bilaterally without dysmetria. No intention tremor. Rapid alternating movements mildly slow bilaterally.
neurological_exam.gait: Normal gait, tandem walk intact. Romberg negative.
neurological_exam.additional: No paratonic rigidity. No myoclonus. No primitive reflexes (grasp, snout, palmomental). Anosmia not formally tested.

patient.chief_complaint: Progressive memory problems and getting lost while driving
patient.history_present_illness: A 62-year-old right-handed retired civil engineer presents to the neurology outpatient clinic with a one-year history of progressive memory difficulties, brought in by his wife who has been increasingly concerned about his cognitive function. The patient initially noticed difficulty remembering the names of colleagues at professional gatherings approximately 14 months ago, which he attributed to normal aging and retirement-related disengagement. Over the following months, his wife observed that he began repeating questions within the same conversation, sometimes asking the same thing three or four times in an hour. He has had increasing difficulty managing the household finances, which he had always handled independently, and his wife discovered several unpaid bills and a significant accounting error in their checking account about six months ago. She has since taken over financial management. The patient reports intermittent episodes of visual misperceptions, particularly in low-light conditions, where he describes seeing fleeting shapes in his peripheral vision that he initially dismissed as shadows. He has gotten lost twice on familiar driving routes over the past three months, once requiring his wife to guide him home by phone. He occasionally has difficulty finding words during conversation, substituting with vague terms or pausing mid-sentence. He can still perform basic activities of daily living independently, including dressing, grooming, and eating, but his wife notes he now needs reminders for medications and appointments. His sleep has been disrupted, with frequent nocturnal awakenings and occasional confusion upon waking. He has been using zolpidem intermittently for the past several months. The patient denies any headaches, seizures, focal weakness, numbness, or gait disturbance. He denies depressed mood but his wife reports he has become more withdrawn and apathetic over the past six months, spending most of the day watching television rather than pursuing his former hobbies of woodworking and reading. There has been no recent illness, fever, weight loss, or urinary symptoms.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 8 Hz alpha activity, symmetric, reactive to eye opening but at the lower limit of normal frequency
background.anterior_background: Low to moderate amplitude mixed theta and beta activity
background.sleep_architecture: Not assessed, patient awake throughout recording
background.symmetry: Symmetric background activity without lateralized abnormalities

findings.location: Bilateral, predominantly temporal and parietal regions
findings.state: Awake, more prominent with eyes closed

findings.morphology: Sharp contour with following slow wave, moderate amplitude

artifacts.description: Intermittent, not significantly obscuring the record

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Not performed due to patient age and comorbidities
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: Normal photic driving response, no photoparoxysmal response

eeg.impression: Mildly abnormal EEG showing borderline slow posterior dominant rhythm at 8 Hz and intermittent diffuse theta slowing, predominantly over temporal and parietal regions. Occasional left temporal sharp transients of uncertain clinical significance. No epileptiform discharges or periodic patterns. Findings are nonspecific and may be seen in early neurodegenerative processes, metabolic encephalopathy, or medication effects. Clinical correlation recommended.
eeg.limitations: Routine 30-minute recording; prolonged or sleep-deprived EEG may yield additional information

findings.location: Bilateral medial temporal lobes, right slightly greater than left
findings.size: Scheltens grade 1-2 on right, grade 1 on left

signal_characteristics.T1: Mildly prominent temporal horns of lateral ventricles suggesting hippocampal volume loss
signal_characteristics.T2_FLAIR: No signal abnormality within the hippocampal formations

findings.location: Bilateral parietal cortices with subtle temporoparietal predominance

signal_characteristics.T1: Mild widening of sulci in parietal regions

findings.location: Periventricular and scattered deep white matter, bilateral

signal_characteristics.T2_FLAIR: Confluent periventricular hyperintensities with scattered punctate deep white matter foci
signal_characteristics.T1: Subtle hypointensity in some periventricular areas

findings.borders: Poorly defined, typical of chronic small vessel disease

findings.location: Small arachnoid cyst, left middle cranial fossa

volumetrics.hippocampal_volume: Left: 2.8 mL (12th percentile for age), Right: 2.5 mL (7th percentile for age)
volumetrics.total_brain_volume: 1085 mL (22nd percentile for age and sex)

additional_observations: No mass lesions or areas of enhancement post-gadolinium
Major intracranial vessels patent on MRA
Mastoid air cells and paranasal sinuses clear
mri.impression: Mild bilateral hippocampal volume loss (Scheltens grade 1-2), slightly more pronounced on the right, with subtle temporoparietal cortical atrophy. Incidental left middle cranial fossa arachnoid cyst. No restricted diffusion, acute infarcts, or mass lesions. The hippocampal atrophy is borderline for the patient's age and could represent early neurodegenerative change, though the concurrent white matter disease raises the possibility of a vascular contribution to cognitive decline. Clinical correlation recommended.

findings: Left ventricular hypertrophy by voltage criteria (Sokolow-Lyon 38 mm)
Nonspecific ST-T wave changes in lateral leads (I, aVL, V5-V6)
ecg.interpretation: Normal sinus rhythm at 74 bpm. Left ventricular hypertrophy by voltage criteria with associated repolarization changes. No arrhythmia.

labs.interpretation: Abnormal values: Glucose 118 mg/dL (H), TSH 5.8 mIU/L (H), HbA1c 6.9 % (H).

Neuropsychological Testing Summary.test: Rey Auditory Verbal Learning Test - Immediate
Rey Auditory Verbal Learning Test - Delayed

abnormal_values_summary: Trail Making B 168 seconds (markedly impaired executive function)

signal_characteristics.Frontal: Moderate tracer retention in prefrontal and lateral frontal cortices
signal_characteristics.Parietal: Moderate tracer retention, bilateral temporoparietal cortices
signal_characteristics.Temporal: Moderate tracer retention in lateral temporal cortices

additional_observations: No focal areas of markedly asymmetric uptake
output.impression: POSITIVE amyloid PET scan. Moderate diffuse cortical amyloid deposition with temporoparietal predominance. Note that amyloid positivity alone does not establish clinical Alzheimer's disease and must be correlated with clinical syndrome and other biomarkers. Clinical correlation recommended.

signal_characteristics.Bilateral_temporoparietal: Moderate hypometabolism in bilateral posterior temporoparietal cortices, right greater than left
signal_characteristics.Posterior_cingulate: Moderate hypometabolism in posterior cingulate and precuneus

additional_observations: Posterior cingulate/precuneus involvement is an early and characteristic finding in AD
Preserved primary cortices and subcortical structures
output.impression: Frontal, primary sensorimotor, and occipital cortices are relatively preserved. Clinical correlation recommended.

output.interpretation: Opening pressure: 14 cmH2O. WBC: 1 cell/uL (normal) (N/A PMN/N/A lymph). Protein: 38 mg/dL (normal). Glucose: 62 mg/dL (normal) (ratio 0.53 (normal)). Amyloid_beta_42: 412 pg/mL (decreased; reference >500 pg/mL). Amyloid_beta_40: 8200 pg/mL (normal). Abeta42_40_ratio: 0.050 (decreased; reference >0.062). Phospho_tau_181: 68 pg/mL (elevated; reference <60 pg/mL). Total_tau: 485 pg/mL (mildly elevated; reference <400 pg/mL). 14-3-3_protein: Negative. RT-QuIC: Negative. NMDA_receptor_antibodies: Negative. VGKC_complex_antibodies: Negative. Oligoclonal_bands: None detected. IgG_index: 0.58 (normal).

abnormal_values_summary: APOE e3/e4 heterozygous carrier (increased AD risk)

output.query: early Alzheimer's disease diagnostic criteria NIA-AA biomarker framework

results.title: NIA-AA Research Framework: Toward a biological definition of Alzheimer's disease
results.authors: Jack CR Jr, Bennett DA, Blennow K, et al.
results.key_finding: AT(N) framework classifies AD biomarkers into three groups: Amyloid (A), Tau (T), and Neurodegeneration (N). Diagnosis requires A+ and T+ for AD biological definition.

results.title: Appropriate use criteria for amyloid PET: A report of the Amyloid Imaging Task Force
results.authors: Johnson KA, Minoshima S, Bohnen NI, et al.
results.key_finding: Amyloid PET is appropriate for patients with persistent or progressive unexplained MCI, atypical presentations, or early-onset progressive dementia. Not recommended for asymptomatic screening.

results.title: Clinical and biomarker changes in dominantly inherited Alzheimer's disease
results.authors: Bateman RJ, Xiong C, Benzinger TL, et al.
results.key_finding: Biomarker changes in AD follow a temporal sequence: amyloid accumulation (15-25 years before symptoms), tau pathology (10-15 years before), neurodegeneration (5-10 years before), then cognitive decline.

output.summary: Current diagnostic criteria for Alzheimer's disease incorporate the NIA-AA AT(N) biomarker framework. A positive amyloid biomarker (decreased CSF Abeta42 or positive amyloid PET) combined with a positive tau biomarker (elevated CSF phospho-tau or positive tau PET) defines AD on the Alzheimer's continuum. Neurodegeneration markers (elevated total tau, FDG-PET hypometabolism, MRI atrophy) provide staging information. For clinical diagnosis of MCI due to AD or AD dementia, biomarker evidence of amyloid and tau pathology should be combined with appropriate clinical syndrome.

interactions: Donepezil + Zolpidem: Both have CNS depressant effects. Combined use may increase sedation and confusion risk. Consider discontinuing zolpidem if starting donepezil.
Donepezil + Metformin: No significant interaction. Both may be continued safely.
contraindications: Relative contraindication in patients with significant cardiac conduction abnormalities (patient's ECG shows no conduction disease)
warnings: Cholinesterase inhibitors may cause bradycardia; monitor heart rate given patient's amlodipine use
GI side effects (nausea, diarrhea, appetite loss) common, especially at initiation
May worsen obstructive sleep apnea symptoms via increased cholinergic activity
output.formulary_status: Tier 1 generic, covered by most insurance plans
alternatives: Rivastigmine (transdermal patch may have fewer GI side effects)
Memantine (for moderate-to-severe AD, can be combined with ChEI)

output.query: differentiating vascular cognitive impairment from Alzheimer's disease white matter hyperintensities

results.title: Diagnostic criteria for vascular cognitive disorders: A VASCOG statement
results.authors: Sachdev P, Kalaria R, O'Brien J, et al.
results.key_finding: VCI diagnosis requires both cognitive impairment AND significant cerebrovascular disease. Cognitive profile typically shows prominent executive dysfunction with relatively preserved episodic memory, unlike AD.

results.title: White matter hyperintensities and cognition: Testing the reserve hypothesis
results.authors: Brickman AM, Siedlecki KL, Muraskin J, et al.
results.key_finding: WMH burden correlates with executive function and processing speed but not strongly with episodic memory. Fazekas grade 2 WMH are common in elderly with cardiovascular risk factors and may coexist with AD.

output.summary: Differentiating VCI from AD involves evaluating the cognitive profile (executive dysfunction vs episodic memory impairment), temporal course (stepwise vs gradual), imaging pattern (strategic infarcts/extensive WMH vs hippocampal atrophy), and vascular risk factor burden. Mixed pathology (AD + VCI) is common in elderly patients with cardiovascular risk factors. Fazekas grade 2 WMH alone does not establish VCI as the primary etiology; amyloid and tau biomarkers can help distinguish AD from pure VCI.

differential.key_distinguishing: Fazekas grade 2 WMH and cardiovascular risk factors suggest vascular contribution, but cognitive profile shows prominent episodic memory impairment rather than the executive dysfunction pattern typical of VCI. Positive amyloid and tau biomarkers confirm AD pathology.
White matter disease may contribute to overall cognitive burden. Cannot exclude mixed pathology; however, primary cognitive profile is most consistent with AD.
TSH mildly elevated at 5.8 with normal free T4. Subclinical hypothyroidism alone unlikely to account for the degree and pattern of cognitive deficits seen on neuropsychological testing.

GDS score borderline. Apathy present but onset followed cognitive decline rather than preceding it. Neuropsychological pattern shows true encoding and storage deficits inconsistent with effort-dependent performance seen in depression.

Zolpidem use could contribute to confusion and memory complaints. However, progressive course over 14 months and structural/biomarker evidence argues against medication as primary etiology. Discontinuation should be considered regardless.

Patient has visual misperceptions but not formed visual hallucinations. No parkinsonism on exam. No REM sleep behavior disorder reported. No significant fluctuations in alertness. FDG-PET does not show occipital hypometabolism.

optimal_actions.action: Obtain brain MRI to evaluate for structural causes and atrophy pattern
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Mild bilateral hippocampal atrophy (Scheltens 1-2) with temporoparietal cortical volume loss and moderate white matter disease

optimal_actions.action: Order comprehensive laboratory workup including CBC, BMP, thyroid function, B12, folate, RPR, HIV
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal metabolic workup except mildly elevated TSH and suboptimal diabetic control; rules out reversible causes of dementia

optimal_actions.action: Request formal neuropsychological testing to characterize cognitive profile
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Amnestic MCI to early dementia profile with prominent episodic memory impairment, impaired executive function, and reduced semantic fluency consistent with AD pattern

optimal_actions.action: Obtain EEG to evaluate for subclinical seizures or other electrographic abnormalities
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Borderline slow PDR and mild diffuse theta slowing consistent with early neurodegenerative process

optimal_actions.action: Request amyloid PET or CSF biomarkers to confirm amyloid pathology
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Positive amyloid PET or decreased CSF Abeta42 with elevated phospho-tau confirming AD pathology

optimal_actions.action: Search literature on AD diagnostic criteria and differentiation from vascular cognitive impairment
optimal_actions.expected_finding: NIA-AA AT(N) framework supporting biomarker-based AD diagnosis

optimal_actions.action: Check drug interactions before initiating cholinesterase inhibitor therapy
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Interaction with zolpidem; caution with bradycardia risk; recommend discontinuing zolpidem

optimal_actions.action: Request APOE genotyping for risk stratification
optimal_actions.expected_finding: APOE e3/e4 heterozygous carrier supporting increased AD risk

critical_actions: Order brain MRI to assess for hippocampal atrophy and exclude structural lesions
Obtain comprehensive labs to rule out reversible causes of cognitive decline (thyroid, B12, RPR, HIV)
Perform or request formal neuropsychological testing to characterize the cognitive deficit pattern
Obtain amyloid biomarker (amyloid PET or CSF Abeta42) to confirm Alzheimer's pathology given borderline MRI findings
Assess for and recommend discontinuation of zolpidem which may be exacerbating cognitive symptoms
Evaluate subclinical hypothyroidism and consider treatment if contributing to symptoms
contraindicated_actions: Diagnosing Alzheimer's disease based solely on clinical presentation without biomarker confirmation, especially given competing vascular pathology
Attributing cognitive decline primarily to subclinical hypothyroidism without further workup
Initiating anticholinergic medications which would worsen cognitive symptoms
Performing lumbar puncture without first obtaining brain MRI to rule out mass lesions
Starting memantine as initial monotherapy (indicated for moderate-to-severe AD, not early-stage)
key_reasoning_points: Progressive episodic memory loss with functional decline (bills, driving) over 14 months in a 62-year-old raises concern for early-onset neurodegenerative disease, most commonly Alzheimer's disease
MoCA of 21/30 with prominent deficits in delayed recall (0/5) and executive function is consistent with amnestic MCI or early AD dementia

MRI showing borderline hippocampal atrophy (Scheltens 1-2) combined with Fazekas grade 2 WMH creates diagnostic ambiguity between AD and vascular cognitive impairment, necessitating biomarker confirmation
Mildly elevated TSH (5.8) with normal free T4 represents subclinical hypothyroidism - an important reversible cause to evaluate but unlikely to fully explain the cognitive profile
Zolpidem use is a confounding factor that could contribute to memory complaints and confusion; should be discontinued as part of workup
Visual misperceptions raise the differential of Lewy body dementia, but absence of parkinsonism, formed hallucinations, and REM sleep behavior disorder makes this less likely
Neuropsychological testing showing severe delayed recall impairment with rapid forgetting is highly characteristic of medial temporal/hippocampal dysfunction seen in AD
Positive amyloid biomarkers (amyloid PET or decreased CSF Abeta42) combined with elevated tau in the context of amnestic cognitive syndrome confirm the diagnosis using NIA-AA AT(N) criteria
APOE e4 carrier status increases pre-test probability but is not diagnostic; used for risk stratification

The original source case presented with CJD-like features (rapid decline, visual hallucinations, DWI changes); this case is adapted to early AD with a more gradual trajectory and biomarker-confirmed amyloid pathology

metadata.difficulty_description: Moderate difficulty: borderline MRI findings with concurrent white matter disease creating ambiguity between AD and vascular cognitive impairment, incidental subclinical hypothyroidism as a confounding factor, zolpidem use as a potential contributor to cognitive complaints, and visual misperceptions raising the differential of Lewy body dementia. Biomarker confirmation is required to establish the diagnosis definitively.
